questions.question: Agriculture is best defined as:

options.B: the art and science of cultivating the soil, raising crops, and rearing livestock for human benefit
options.C: the management of forests and wildlife
options.D: the processing and distribution of food products

questions.explanation: Agriculture encompasses all activities involved in cultivating land, growing crops, and raising animals for food, fibre, and other products for human use.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a branch of agriculture?

options.B: Animal husbandry

questions.explanation: Geology is the study of rocks and the Earth's structure — it is not a branch of agriculture. Branches of agriculture include crop production, animal husbandry, aquaculture, forestry, and agricultural engineering.

questions.question: Subsistence farming is a system where:

options.A: large quantities of food are produced for export
options.B: food is produced mainly for the farmer's own consumption with little surplus
options.D: farming is done entirely by machines

questions.explanation: Subsistence farming produces just enough food for the farmer's household with little or no surplus for sale. It is the dominant form of farming in rural Nigeria.

questions.question: Commercial farming differs from subsistence farming in that commercial farming:

options.B: produces crops and animals primarily for sale in the market
options.D: is practiced only in small plots

questions.explanation: Commercial farming is large-scale, profit-oriented agriculture where crops and livestock are produced primarily for sale, using modern inputs, machinery, and improved varieties.

questions.question: Mixed farming involves:

options.B: combining crop cultivation with livestock rearing on the same farm
options.C: farming on both wet and dry land
options.D: using both organic and inorganic fertilizers

questions.explanation: Mixed farming integrates crop production and animal husbandry. Crop residues feed animals; animal manure fertilizes crops, creating a beneficial cycle.

questions.question: Soil is formed through the process of:

options.B: weathering of parent rock material and decomposition of organic matter over time
options.D: evaporation of water from the ground

questions.explanation: Soil formation (pedogenesis) involves the physical and chemical weathering of parent rock material, decomposition of organic matter, and the activity of living organisms over thousands of years.

questions.question: The three main soil particles in order of decreasing size are:

options.B: Sand, Silt, Clay

questions.explanation: Soil particle sizes: Sand (0.05-2mm) > Silt (0.002-0.05mm) > Clay (<0.002mm). Sandy soils drain fast; clay soils retain water and nutrients but may become waterlogged.

questions.question: Loam soil is considered the best agricultural soil because it:

options.B: is a mixture of sand, silt, and clay with good water retention and drainage

questions.explanation: Loam soil combines sand (drainage), silt (nutrient retention), and clay (water and nutrient retention) in balanced proportions, making it ideal for most crops.

questions.question: Soil pH measures the:

options.B: acidity or alkalinity of the soil on a scale of 1-14

questions.explanation: Soil pH measures acidity (pH below 7) or alkalinity (pH above 7). Most crops grow best in slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6.0–7.0). pH affects nutrient availability and microbial activity.

questions.question: Liming of acidic soil is done to:

options.B: raise soil pH and improve nutrient availability

questions.explanation: Liming (applying calcium carbonate/limestone) neutralizes soil acidity, raising the pH. This improves nutrient availability (especially phosphorus) and creates better conditions for soil bacteria.

questions.question: Humus is important in soil because it:

options.A: makes the soil hard and compact
options.B: improves soil structure, water retention, and nutrient supply

questions.explanation: Humus (decomposed organic matter) improves soil structure by binding particles, increases water-holding capacity, provides nutrients, and supports beneficial soil organisms.

questions.question: The main agents of soil erosion are:

options.B: Water (rainfall and runoff) and wind

questions.explanation: The primary agents of soil erosion are water (raindrop impact, surface runoff) and wind. Erosion removes the fertile topsoil, reducing agricultural productivity.

questions.question: Contour farming as a method of soil conservation involves:

options.A: growing crops in rows running up and down the slope
options.B: ploughing and planting across the slope along contour lines
options.C: burning crop residues to prevent erosion
options.D: applying chemical pesticides to stabilize soil

questions.explanation: Contour farming involves ploughing and planting along the contour (across the slope) rather than up and down. This creates ridges that slow water runoff and trap soil.

questions.question: Terracing as a soil conservation method is most effective on:

options.B: steep slopes where it creates level platforms for cultivation

questions.explanation: Terracing cuts a series of level steps (terraces) into steep hillsides, reducing runoff speed and erosion. It is widely used in Nigeria's southeastern states and other hilly areas.

questions.question: The three primary (major) macronutrients essential for plant growth are:

options.B: Calcium (Ca), Magnesium (Mg), Sulfur (S)

questions.explanation: NPK — Nitrogen (promotes leafy growth), Phosphorus (root and flower development), and Potassium (disease resistance, fruit quality) are the three primary macronutrients in fertilizers.

questions.question: Nitrogen deficiency in plants causes:

options.B: yellowing of older leaves (chlorosis) and stunted growth

questions.explanation: Nitrogen deficiency causes yellowing (chlorosis) of older leaves first (nitrogen is mobile and moves from old to new leaves), stunted growth, and pale green coloration.

questions.question: Phosphorus deficiency in plants typically causes:

options.B: purple or reddish coloration of leaves and poor root development

questions.explanation: Phosphorus deficiency causes a characteristic purple or reddish discoloration of leaves (due to anthocyanin accumulation), poor root development, and delayed maturity.

questions.question: Organic fertilizers differ from inorganic (chemical) fertilizers in that organic fertilizers:

options.B: are derived from living or once-living material and improve soil structure

questions.explanation: Organic fertilizers (manure, compost, bone meal) are derived from organic matter, release nutrients slowly, improve soil structure, and support soil microbial life.

questions.question: Urea is a nitrogenous fertilizer that contains approximately:

options.B: 46% nitrogen

questions.explanation: Urea (CO(NH₂)₂) contains approximately 46% nitrogen, making it the most concentrated and widely used solid nitrogen fertilizer.

questions.question: Green manuring involves:

options.A: applying chemical fertilizers in liquid form
options.B: growing leguminous plants and ploughing them into the soil while still green to add organic matter

questions.explanation: Green manuring involves growing nitrogen-fixing crops (legumes like cowpea, mucuna) and incorporating them into the soil while still green, adding organic matter and nitrogen.

questions.question: Crop rotation is the practice of:

options.A: rotating the direction of ploughing each year
options.B: growing different crops on the same land in successive seasons to maintain soil fertility
options.C: transplanting crops from one field to another
options.D: growing the same crop every year

questions.explanation: Crop rotation alternates different crops (e.g., cereals, legumes, roots) in sequence on the same land. It prevents nutrient depletion, breaks pest/disease cycles, and improves soil structure.

questions.question: Intercropping is the agricultural practice of:

options.A: growing one crop at a time
options.B: growing two or more crops simultaneously on the same land
options.C: planting crops in between crop rows of a perennial crop

questions.explanation: Intercropping grows two or more crops together at the same time on the same land, maximizing land use, reducing erosion, and providing multiple harvests.

questions.question: The main advantage of shifting cultivation is:

options.B: it allows soil to recover its fertility naturally during the fallow period
options.D: it produces large surpluses for export

questions.explanation: Shifting cultivation allows farmers to move to fresh land when old plots lose fertility, letting them regenerate naturally. The major disadvantages are low productivity and contribution to deforestation.

questions.question: The process of seed germination requires:

options.B: water, oxygen, and suitable temperature

questions.explanation: Seed germination requires: (1) water (to activate enzymes and soften the seed coat), (2) oxygen (for aerobic respiration), and (3) suitable temperature (warmth to activate biochemical processes). Light is not required.

questions.question: Transplanting of seedlings is done in crop production to:

options.B: allow seedlings to develop under controlled conditions before being moved to the field

questions.explanation: Transplanting involves raising seedlings in a nursery under controlled conditions, then moving them to the main field. It saves land, ensures uniform plant stands, and protects early stages.

questions.question: The process of removing extra seedlings from a stand to achieve the desired plant population is called:

options.B: Thinning

questions.explanation: Thinning removes excess seedlings from an overcrowded stand, leaving the desired number of plants at the correct spacing to reduce competition and optimize yields.

questions.question: Mulching in crop production involves:

options.B: covering the soil surface with organic or inorganic materials to conserve moisture and reduce weeds

questions.explanation: Mulching covers the soil around plants with organic materials (straw, leaves, wood chips) or plastic to conserve moisture, moderate soil temperature, suppress weeds, and add organic matter.

questions.question: Ridging in crop production is primarily done to:

options.A: compact the soil for better water retention
options.B: create mounds that improve drainage, aeration, and ease of harvesting root crops
options.C: prevent animals from entering the farm

questions.explanation: Ridging creates raised mounds or furrows, improving soil drainage, aeration, and ease of harvesting root and tuber crops like yam and cassava. It also helps with hilling up plants.

questions.question: Staking in crop production is important for:

options.B: providing support for climbing or tall plants to prevent lodging

questions.explanation: Staking provides physical support (bamboo sticks, poles) for climbing plants (yam, tomato, beans) or tall crops prone to lodging (falling over), improving air circulation and yields.

questions.question: The term 'lodging' in crop production refers to:

options.A: the provision of housing for farm workers
options.B: the bending or falling over of crop stems due to wind, rain, or weak stems
options.D: planting crops too deeply in the soil

questions.explanation: Lodging occurs when crop stems bend or collapse (especially cereals like maize and sorghum) due to strong winds, heavy rain, excessive nitrogen, or weak stems, reducing yields significantly.

questions.question: A cutlass (machete) is primarily used in farm operations for:

options.B: clearing bush, cutting weeds, and harvesting

questions.explanation: The cutlass/machete is the most widely used farm tool in West Africa for clearing bush, cutting weeds, harvesting crops, and general farm clearing.

questions.question: A hoe is mainly used for:

options.B: loosening and turning soil, weeding, and making ridges

questions.explanation: The hoe is a fundamental hand tool used for soil cultivation, making ridges/mounds, weeding, and breaking the soil surface. It is the most common planting tool in subsistence farming.

questions.question: A tractor is classified as:

options.B: a complex/power-driven farm machinery

questions.explanation: Tractors are complex, power-driven machines that provide power for various farm operations (ploughing, harrowing, planting, harvesting) by attaching different implements.

questions.question: A plough is used in farm operations to:

options.B: turn over and break up the soil to prepare a seedbed

questions.explanation: A plough (moldboard, disc, or chisel) is the primary tillage implement used to turn over the upper layer of soil, preparing it for planting by burying weeds and crop residues.

questions.question: A harrow is a farm implement used to:

options.B: break up clods and smooth the soil surface after ploughing

questions.explanation: Harrowing is secondary tillage that breaks up clods left after ploughing, levels the soil surface, and creates a fine, firm seedbed suitable for planting.

questions.question: Proper maintenance of farm tools includes all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: sharpening cutting edges
options.C: oiling metal parts to prevent rust
options.D: leaving tools in the rain after use

questions.explanation: Proper tool maintenance includes: cleaning after use, sharpening blades, oiling metal parts, storing in dry places, and repairing handles. Leaving tools in rain causes rust and deterioration.

questions.question: Primary tillage refers to the:

options.A: final levelling of soil before planting
options.B: initial deep turning or breaking of soil using a plough

questions.explanation: Primary tillage (ploughing) is the first and deepest soil cultivation operation that turns over and loosens the soil. Secondary tillage (harrowing) refines the seedbed.

questions.question: The slash and burn method of land clearing involves:

options.B: cutting down vegetation and burning it to clear land and add ash as fertilizer
options.D: flooding the land to kill weeds

questions.explanation: Slash and burn (swidden agriculture) involves cutting vegetation and burning it. The ash adds potassium and other nutrients, temporarily improving fertility. It is associated with shifting cultivation.

questions.question: Plant breeding aims to develop varieties with:

options.A: lower yields but more colorful flowers
options.B: improved characteristics such as higher yield, disease resistance, and drought tolerance

questions.explanation: Plant breeding develops new crop varieties with improved traits: higher yields, disease and pest resistance, drought tolerance, improved nutritional quality, and better adaptability.

questions.question: Hybridization in plant breeding involves:

options.B: crossing two different varieties or species to produce offspring with desired traits
options.C: selecting the best individual plants for seed
options.D: exposing plants to radiation to induce mutations

questions.explanation: Hybridization is the crossing of genetically different parents (varieties or species) to produce hybrids that combine desirable traits from both parents, often showing hybrid vigor (heterosis).

questions.question: The International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) located in Ibadan, Nigeria, is known for:

options.B: conducting agricultural research and developing improved crop varieties for tropical Africa

questions.explanation: IITA (International Institute of Tropical Agriculture) based in Ibadan is a leading international agricultural research center developing improved varieties of cassava, maize, soybean, and other tropical crops.

questions.question: Drip irrigation is the most efficient method because:

options.A: it applies large amounts of water quickly
options.B: it delivers water slowly and directly to plant roots, minimizing water loss through evaporation

questions.explanation: Drip (trickle) irrigation delivers water drop by drop directly to the plant root zone through emitters. It has up to 95% water use efficiency, far better than flood (50%) or sprinkler (75%) irrigation.

questions.question: Flood (furrow) irrigation is the most common method in Nigeria mainly because:

options.B: it is simple, cheap, and requires little technology

questions.explanation: Flood/furrow irrigation is widely used in Nigeria despite lower efficiency because it is simple to implement, requires no expensive equipment, and uses gravity to distribute water.

questions.question: Weeds are harmful to crops because they:

options.B: compete with crops for nutrients, water, light, and space, reducing yields

questions.explanation: Weeds compete with crops for water, nutrients, light, and space, reducing crop yields. They also harbor pests and diseases, and may release allelopathic chemicals that inhibit crop growth.

questions.question: Striga (witch weed) is a particularly harmful weed because:

options.A: it is toxic to soil organisms
options.B: it is a parasitic weed that attaches to the roots of cereals (maize, sorghum, millet) and extracts nutrients
options.C: it produces thorns that injure farmers

questions.explanation: Striga (witchweed) is a parasitic weed that attaches to cereal roots, extracting water and nutrients. It is a major constraint to cereal production in sub-Saharan Africa.

questions.question: Herbicides are chemicals used for:

options.B: killing or suppressing weeds

questions.explanation: Herbicides are chemical weed killers. They can be selective (killing specific weeds without harming crops) or non-selective (killing all vegetation). Examples: atrazine, glyphosate, 2,4-D.

questions.question: The most reliable method of weed control on small farms in Nigeria is:

options.B: manual weeding with hoe or hand

questions.explanation: Manual weeding (hand-hoeing) is the most widely practiced weed control method on smallholder farms in Nigeria due to low cost, accessibility, and no risk of chemical contamination.

questions.question: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is:

options.B: a combined approach using biological, cultural, physical, and chemical methods to manage pests economically
options.C: total elimination of all insects on the farm
options.D: using only biological methods to control pests

questions.explanation: IPM uses a combination of pest control strategies (biological, cultural, physical, chemical) to reduce pest populations below economically damaging levels while minimizing environmental impact.

questions.question: Biological control of pests involves:

options.B: using natural enemies (predators, parasites, pathogens) to control pest populations

questions.explanation: Biological control uses natural enemies — predatory insects, parasitic wasps, bacteria (Bt), fungi, or viruses — to reduce pest populations. It is environmentally friendly and sustainable.

questions.question: Cassava mosaic disease is caused by a:

options.B: bacterium

questions.explanation: Cassava Mosaic Disease (CMD) is caused by a geminivirus transmitted by the whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). It causes mottled, distorted leaves and can cause severe yield losses.

questions.question: Maize stalk borer (Busseola fusca) is a major pest of maize that damages the crop by:

options.B: boring into the stem and causing deadheart in young plants and broken cobs in older ones

questions.explanation: The stalk borer larva bores into the maize stem, causing 'deadheart' (death of the central shoot) in young plants and 'tassel damage' and broken stems in older plants.

questions.question: The best way to control soil-borne diseases is through:

options.B: crop rotation with non-host crops to break the disease cycle

questions.explanation: Crop rotation with non-host crops effectively controls soil-borne pathogens (fungi, bacteria, nematodes) by breaking their life cycle, as they cannot survive without their specific host plant.

questions.question: Post-harvest losses in crop storage are caused by:

options.B: insects, fungi, rodents, moisture, and improper storage conditions

questions.explanation: Post-harvest losses are caused by insects (weevils, borers), moulds/fungi, rodents, excessive moisture, improper temperature, and lack of proper storage facilities — causing 25-40% losses in Nigeria.

questions.question: Grain weevils (Sitophilus species) are major pests of stored grains. The best method to control them in small-scale storage is:

options.B: mixing dry ash, neem leaves, or approved grain protectant chemicals with stored grain

questions.explanation: Traditional methods like mixing dry wood ash or neem powder, or modern grain protectants (insecticides), effectively control grain weevils in storage. Airtight containers also prevent infestation.

questions.question: Aflatoxin contamination of groundnuts and maize in storage is caused by:

options.B: the fungus Aspergillus flavus growing in humid storage conditions

questions.explanation: Aflatoxins are toxic compounds produced by the fungi Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus, which grow on groundnuts, maize, and other crops stored in warm, humid conditions. They are highly carcinogenic.

questions.question: Cattle in Nigeria are reared mainly for:

options.B: meat, milk, draught power, and hides

questions.explanation: Cattle are multipurpose animals providing beef, milk (dairy), draught power for farming, and hides and skins for leather. They also serve cultural and ceremonial purposes.

questions.question: The White Fulani (Bunaji) is the most common indigenous cattle breed in Nigeria and is known for:

options.B: adaptability to tropical conditions, disease resistance, and dual purpose (meat and milk)

questions.explanation: The White Fulani (Bunaji) is the most numerically dominant cattle breed in Nigeria, valued for its adaptability to tropical heat, disease resistance (especially trypanosomiasis), and dual meat-milk purpose.

questions.question: Ruminant animals like cattle, goats, and sheep have a special stomach called the rumen that enables them to:

options.B: digest fibrous plant material (cellulose) with the help of microorganisms

questions.explanation: Ruminants have a four-chambered stomach (rumen, reticulum, omasum, abomasum). Microorganisms in the rumen ferment and break down cellulose in plant material through a process of chewing the cud (rumination).

questions.question: Mastitis is a disease of cattle that affects the:

options.B: digestive system

questions.explanation: Mastitis is the inflammation of the udder (mammary gland) in dairy cows, usually caused by bacterial infection (Staphylococcus, Streptococcus). It causes reduced milk production and poor milk quality.

questions.question: The two main systems of poultry production are:

options.B: intensive (battery cage, deep litter) and extensive (free range)

questions.explanation: Poultry systems include intensive (battery cages for layers, deep litter for broilers and layers) and extensive/semi-intensive (free-range, backyard) systems, each with different management requirements.

questions.question: Newcastle disease in poultry is caused by a:

options.B: fungus

questions.explanation: Newcastle Disease (ND) is a highly contagious viral disease of poultry caused by the Newcastle disease virus (NDV/avian paramyxovirus type 1). It causes respiratory, nervous, and digestive signs and high mortality.

questions.question: Fowl pox in poultry is characterized by:

options.B: wart-like lesions on the comb, wattles, and face (dry form) or lesions in the mouth/throat (wet form)

questions.explanation: Fowl pox (dry/cutaneous form) causes warty scab lesions on featherless skin (comb, wattles). The wet/diphtheritic form causes lesions in the mouth and throat, making breathing difficult.

questions.question: The incubation period for domestic chicken eggs is approximately:

options.B: 21 days

questions.explanation: Domestic chicken eggs hatch after approximately 21 days of incubation at 37-38°C with proper humidity and turning. Duck eggs take 28 days; quail eggs take 17-18 days.

questions.question: Pigs are monogastric animals, which means they:

options.A: have a four-chambered stomach like cattle
options.B: have a single-chambered (simple) stomach similar to humans

questions.explanation: Pigs are monogastric (single-stomached) animals that cannot digest cellulose. They require a diet high in digestible carbohydrates, proteins, and fat — unlike ruminants.

questions.question: African Swine Fever (ASF) is a highly contagious disease of pigs that:

options.B: has no vaccine and causes up to 100% mortality — the only control is culling

questions.explanation: African Swine Fever (ASF) is a highly lethal viral haemorrhagic disease of pigs with up to 100% mortality. There is currently no effective vaccine; control relies on strict quarantine and culling of infected herds.

questions.question: The West African Dwarf (WAD) goat is significant because:

options.A: it produces the most milk of all African breeds
options.B: it is trypanotolerant, disease resistant, and well adapted to humid tropical conditions
options.C: it has the highest body weight

questions.explanation: The West African Dwarf (WAD) goat is valued for its trypanotolerance, adaptability to the humid tropics, disease resistance, and hardiness on poor-quality feeds.

questions.question: Peste des Petits Ruminants (PPR) is a highly contagious viral disease that primarily affects:

options.B: goats and sheep

questions.explanation: PPR (goat and sheep plague) is a highly contagious viral disease of small ruminants (goats and sheep) causing fever, nasal discharge, pneumonia, diarrhoea, and high mortality. Vaccination is the primary control.

questions.question: Aquaculture is the practice of:

options.B: the controlled cultivation of aquatic organisms (fish, shellfish, algae) in fresh or salt water

questions.explanation: Aquaculture is the farming of fish, shellfish, seaweed, and other aquatic organisms under controlled conditions. Catfish and tilapia farming are the most popular in Nigeria.

questions.question: The most commonly farmed fish species in Nigeria is:

options.A: Tilapia and Catfish (Clarias and Heterobranchus species)
options.B: Salmon

questions.explanation: Catfish (Clarias gariepinus, Heterobranchus species) and tilapia are the most widely farmed fish in Nigeria due to their rapid growth rate, tolerance of poor water quality, and market demand.

questions.question: The energy source in animal feeds is primarily provided by:

options.B: Vitamins

questions.explanation: Carbohydrates (grains: maize, sorghum) and fats are the primary energy sources in animal feeds. Proteins are for growth and tissue repair; vitamins and minerals are for metabolic functions.

questions.question: Protein deficiency in farm animals causes:

options.B: poor growth, reduced milk/egg production, and weakened immune system

questions.explanation: Protein deficiency causes poor growth and weight gain, reduced milk and egg production, impaired reproduction, poor immune response, and edema (fluid swelling) in severe cases.

questions.question: A concentrate feed in animal nutrition is:

options.A: low in energy and high in fiber
options.B: high in energy and/or protein with low fiber content
options.D: a vitamin and mineral supplement only

questions.explanation: Concentrates are feeds high in energy (grain: maize, wheat bran) or protein (soybean meal, groundnut cake) with low fibre content, used to supplement roughages (forages/grasses).

questions.question: Rinderpest (cattle plague) was a major viral disease of cattle that has been:

options.A: still a major problem in Nigeria
options.B: globally eradicated through vaccination campaigns (declared eradicated in 2011)
options.C: replaced by a more dangerous disease

questions.explanation: Rinderpest was declared globally eradicated in 2011 by the FAO and OIE — only the second disease ever eradicated (after human smallpox). Global vaccination campaigns achieved this historic victory.

questions.question: Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD) in cattle is characterized by:

options.B: fever, blisters on the mouth, tongue, and feet, causing lameness

questions.explanation: FMD (Foot-and-Mouth Disease) is a highly contagious viral disease causing fever, vesicles (blisters) on the mouth/tongue/feet/teats, excessive salivation, and lameness. It spreads rapidly and causes severe economic losses.

questions.question: Trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness in cattle, nagana) is transmitted by the:

options.B: tick

questions.explanation: Trypanosomiasis (nagana in livestock, sleeping sickness in humans) is caused by Trypanosoma species transmitted by the bite of infected tsetse flies (Glossina species). It is a major constraint to livestock production in the tsetse belt.

questions.question: Anthrax is a bacterial disease caused by Bacillus anthracis and is characterized by:

options.A: chronic wasting and gradual weight loss
options.B: sudden death in animals with bloody discharge from body openings

questions.explanation: Anthrax causes sudden death (peracute form) in cattle and horses with bloating, bloody discharge from body orifices, and failure of blood to clot. Carcasses must NOT be opened due to contamination risk.

questions.question: Coccidiosis in poultry is caused by a:

options.B: bacterium

questions.explanation: Coccidiosis is caused by Eimeria protozoa that invade intestinal cells of poultry. It causes bloody diarrhea, weight loss, and high mortality in young chicks. Anticoccidials in feed are used for prevention.

questions.question: Ticks are ectoparasites (external parasites) of cattle that cause harm by:

options.A: blocking the nose and breathing passages
options.B: sucking blood, transmitting diseases, and causing tick worry and weight loss

questions.explanation: Ticks suck large amounts of blood (causing anaemia), transmit diseases (East Coast Fever, babesiosis, anaplasmosis), cause skin damage, and induce restlessness (tick worry), all reducing livestock productivity.

questions.question: Liver fluke (Fasciola hepatica) in cattle and sheep is an endoparasite (internal parasite) that:

options.B: damages the liver and bile ducts, causing bottle jaw and wasting

questions.explanation: Liver fluke (Fasciola hepatica) is a parasitic flatworm that damages the liver and bile ducts of cattle, sheep, and goats, causing fasciolosis characterized by anaemia, bottle jaw (oedema), and emaciation.

questions.question: The law of diminishing returns in farming states that:

options.A: increasing all inputs equally always increases output
options.B: as more units of a variable input are added to fixed inputs, the additional output eventually decreases
options.C: output always decreases as farm size increases
options.D: profits always fall as production increases

questions.explanation: The law of diminishing returns: adding successive units of a variable input (e.g., fertilizer) to fixed inputs (land) will initially increase output, but each additional unit eventually produces less additional output.

questions.question: Farm record keeping is important because it helps the farmer to:

options.A: reduce the number of workers needed
options.B: track income and expenses, plan production, and determine profitability

questions.explanation: Farm records (input records, output records, financial records) help farmers track costs and revenues, plan future production, obtain loans, pay taxes, and determine whether the farm is profitable.

questions.question: A farm budget is a:

options.B: plan showing expected income and expenditure for a farming season

questions.explanation: A farm budget is a financial plan that estimates income (from crop/livestock sales) and expenditure (seeds, fertilizers, labor, etc.) for a farming period, helping in decision-making.

questions.question: Agricultural cooperative societies help farmers by:

options.B: pooling resources to buy inputs cheaply, access credit, and market produce collectively
options.D: providing land to farmers for free

questions.explanation: Agricultural cooperatives allow small farmers to pool resources for bulk purchasing of inputs at lower prices, access credit facilities, collectively market their produce, and share technical knowledge.

questions.question: Agricultural extension services are provided to farmers to:

options.B: educate and assist farmers in adopting improved agricultural technologies and practices

questions.explanation: Agricultural extension services link research institutions to farmers, transferring improved technologies (varieties, practices, management) through demonstrations, farm visits, and training.

questions.question: The main problems of agricultural marketing in Nigeria include all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: lack of storage facilities
options.C: too many buyers competing for produce
options.D: exploitation by middlemen and price fluctuations

questions.explanation: Agricultural marketing in Nigeria is hampered by poor roads, inadequate storage, perishability of produce, price exploitation by middlemen, and lack of market information. Too many buyers would actually benefit farmers.

questions.question: Farm management is the process of:

options.B: making and implementing decisions to organize and operate a farm profitably
options.C: managing only the financial aspects of farming

questions.explanation: Farm management involves planning, organizing, directing, and controlling all farm resources (land, labour, capital, management) to achieve maximum profit and efficiency.

questions.question: Opportunity cost in farm management refers to:

options.A: the cost of labor on the farm
options.B: the value of the next best alternative foregone when making a resource allocation decision

questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the forgone benefit of the next best alternative when resources are allocated to one use. For example, land used for maize cannot simultaneously be used for cassava.

questions.question: A farm map shows:

options.A: only the crops grown on the farm
options.B: the layout of the farm including fields, buildings, roads, and natural features
options.C: the financial records of the farm
options.D: the soil types of the farm

questions.explanation: A farm map is a scaled diagram showing the physical layout of the farm: field boundaries, farm buildings, roads, water bodies, drainage, and other features essential for farm planning.

questions.question: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants:

options.A: break down food to release energy
options.B: convert light energy, water, and CO₂ into glucose and oxygen

questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose) + 6O₂. It occurs in chloroplasts using chlorophyll to capture light energy.

questions.question: Transpiration in plants is the loss of water vapour mainly through:

options.B: stomata (pores) on the leaves

questions.explanation: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant surfaces, mainly through stomata on leaves. It drives the upward movement of water and minerals through the xylem (transpiration pull).

questions.question: The function of roots in a plant includes all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: anchoring the plant in the soil
options.B: absorbing water and minerals
options.D: storage of food in some plants

questions.explanation: Roots do not carry out photosynthesis (they lack chlorophyll and are underground). Root functions: anchorage, absorption of water/minerals, storage (cassava, carrot), and sometimes vegetative reproduction.

questions.question: The main function of flowers in plants is:

options.B: sexual reproduction — producing seeds through pollination and fertilization

questions.explanation: Flowers are the reproductive organs of angiosperms (flowering plants). They facilitate sexual reproduction through pollination, fertilization, and seed/fruit production.

questions.question: Cereal crops are:

options.A: members of the grass family grown for their edible grains
options.B: leguminous crops grown for their seeds
options.C: crops grown for their underground tubers
options.D: crops grown for their edible leaves

questions.explanation: Cereals are grass-family (Gramineae/Poaceae) crops cultivated for their starchy, edible seeds (grains). Examples in Nigeria: maize, sorghum, millet, rice, wheat.

questions.question: Leguminous crops are important in agriculture primarily because:

options.B: they fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule bacteria (Rhizobium)
options.C: they grow faster than other crops

questions.explanation: Legumes (cowpea, soybean, groundnut) host Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules that fix atmospheric nitrogen into plant-available forms, enriching soil nitrogen and reducing need for nitrogen fertilizers.

questions.question: Tuber crops store food (starch) in their:

options.B: stems

questions.explanation: Tuber crops store starch in swollen underground structures: true tubers (yam, potato — swollen stems), root tubers (cassava, sweet potato — swollen roots), and corms (cocoyam).

questions.question: Cassava (Manihot esculenta) is an important food crop in Nigeria because:

options.A: it is a major source of protein
options.B: it is drought-tolerant, grows on poor soils, and is a major source of carbohydrates
options.C: it is the only crop grown in the rainforest

questions.explanation: Cassava is drought-tolerant, grows on poor, acidic soils, produces year-round, and is a major source of carbohydrates for millions of Nigerians. Nigeria is the world's largest cassava producer.

questions.question: Cassava is propagated (planted) using:

options.B: stem cuttings

questions.explanation: Cassava is vegetatively propagated using stem cuttings (stakes) 25-30 cm long. It is rarely grown from true seeds (mainly in breeding programs).

questions.question: Maize (Zea mays) is a crop that is pollinated by:

options.B: water

questions.explanation: Maize is wind-pollinated (anemophilous). The tassel (male flower at the top) releases pollen that is carried by wind to the silk (female flowers) of other plants.

questions.question: Groundnut (Arachis hypogaea) is unique among food crops because its:

options.B: flowers are pollinated underground
options.C: flowers are pollinated above ground but pods develop underground (geocarpy)

questions.explanation: Groundnut (peanut) flowers above ground, but after fertilization the flower stalk (peg/gynophore) curves downward, penetrating the soil where the pod develops underground — a process called geocarpy.

questions.question: The most widely cultivated yam species in Nigeria is:

options.B: Dioscorea rotundata (white Guinea yam)

questions.explanation: Dioscorea rotundata (white Guinea yam) is the most widely cultivated and economically important yam species in Nigeria, accounting for the majority of production.

questions.question: Rice (Oryza sativa) cultivation in Nigeria is concentrated mainly in:

options.B: inland valley swamps, irrigated fields (north), and coastal areas

questions.explanation: Rice is grown in three main ecosystems in Nigeria: upland (rainforest belt), lowland/inland valley swamps (central Nigeria), and irrigated fields (Benue, Niger, Kano, Kebbi rivers). Nigeria is Africa's largest rice consumer.

questions.question: Oil palm (Elaeis guineensis) produces two types of oil:

options.A: Groundnut oil and palm kernel oil
options.B: Palm oil (from the mesocarp/flesh) and palm kernel oil (from the kernel/seed)
options.C: Crude oil and refined oil only

questions.explanation: Oil palm produces: (1) palm oil — red oil extracted from the mesocarp (fleshy part of the fruit), and (2) palm kernel oil — extracted from the kernel (seed inside the hard nut).

questions.question: The main growing area for cocoa in Nigeria is:

options.B: the southwestern and Cross River states

questions.explanation: Cocoa production in Nigeria is concentrated in the southwestern states (Ondo, Ogun, Ekiti, Osun, Oyo) and Cross River State, where the humid forest climate is suitable.

questions.question: The post-harvest processing of cocoa involves:

options.B: pod breaking, fermenting, and drying the cocoa beans before export

questions.explanation: Cocoa processing: pods are broken to extract wet beans → fermented (5-7 days to develop flavor) → dried (sun or artificial drying to 7-8% moisture) → bagged for export or further processing.

questions.question: Cotton (Gossypium species) is grown in Nigeria mainly for:

options.B: fibre for textile industries and cottonseed oil

questions.explanation: Cotton is a cash crop cultivated mainly in northern and middle belt states for cotton lint (textile fiber) and cottonseed (oil for cooking; cake as animal feed).

questions.question: Legume pasture crops are preferred in mixed farming systems because they:

options.B: provide high-protein fodder and fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility

questions.explanation: Legume pastures (Stylosanthes, Centrosema, cowpea) fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, improving soil fertility, and provide high-protein feed for livestock compared to pure grass pastures.

questions.question: Napier grass (Pennisetum purpureum, elephant grass) is important in livestock farming because:

options.B: it is a high-yielding perennial fodder grass that provides large amounts of biomass for feed

questions.explanation: Napier grass (elephant grass) is a tall, fast-growing perennial grass widely used as cut-and-carry fodder for dairy cattle, goats, and rabbits. It produces high biomass and can be harvested multiple times per year.

questions.question: The Agricultural Credit Guarantee Scheme Fund (ACGSF) in Nigeria was established to:

options.B: guarantee agricultural loans made by commercial banks, reducing lending risk and improving farmer access to credit

questions.explanation: The ACGSF (established 1977) guarantees loans to farmers from commercial and merchant banks, encouraging banks to lend to the agricultural sector by reducing default risk.

questions.question: Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) was an agricultural program launched in Nigeria in:
questions.explanation: Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) was launched in 1976 under General Obasanjo's government to boost food production and achieve self-sufficiency. It encouraged urban dwellers to engage in farming.

questions.question: The Green Revolution program introduced during the 2nd Republic under President Shagari was aimed at:

options.B: modernizing agriculture and achieving food self-sufficiency through improved inputs and irrigation

questions.explanation: The Green Revolution (1980-1983) aimed to transform Nigerian agriculture through introduction of high-yielding seed varieties, chemical inputs, mechanization, and expanded irrigation under the River Basin Development Authorities.

questions.question: Waterlogging in agricultural soils is harmful because it:

options.B: creates anaerobic conditions that prevent root respiration and cause root rot

questions.explanation: Waterlogging saturates soil pores with water, excluding oxygen. This creates anaerobic conditions where roots cannot respire properly, leading to root rot, nutrient deficiency, and plant death.

questions.question: Drainage of agricultural land is important to:

options.B: remove excess water, improving soil aeration and workability

questions.explanation: Drainage removes excess water from waterlogged soils, restoring soil aeration, improving root development, allowing earlier cultivation, and preventing root diseases.

questions.question: The best time to harvest maize for grain is when:

options.B: the cobs are fully mature, husks have dried, and grain moisture is below 13-15%

questions.explanation: Maize for grain should be harvested when fully mature and dry (grain moisture 13-15%). Harvesting too early gives soft, immature grain; too late risks pest damage and aflatoxin contamination.

questions.question: Yam is ready for harvest when:

options.B: the leaves and vines begin to yellow and die back (senescence)

questions.explanation: Yam is ready for harvest when the leaves turn yellow and the vines die back, indicating maturity. This typically occurs 7-10 months after planting, depending on the variety.

questions.question: Artificial Insemination (AI) in livestock farming involves:

options.B: collecting semen from a superior sire and depositing it into the female's reproductive tract

questions.explanation: AI involves collecting, storing, and depositing semen into the female's uterus, allowing one superior bull to sire thousands of offspring, improving genetic quality without transporting bulls.

questions.question: The gestation period of cattle is approximately:

options.B: 5 months

questions.explanation: Cattle have a gestation period of approximately 9 months (280-285 days). Compare: goat/sheep = 5 months, pig = 3 months 3 weeks 3 days (3-3-3 rule), rabbit = 31 days.

questions.question: Oestrus (heat) in female animals refers to the period when:

options.B: the female is sexually receptive and can conceive

questions.explanation: Oestrus (heat) is the period of sexual receptivity in female animals when ovulation occurs and conception is possible. Signs include restlessness, mounting other animals, and vulval discharge.

questions.question: Rotational grazing involves:

options.A: keeping animals in one paddock all year
options.B: moving animals between multiple paddocks in rotation, allowing pastures to recover
options.C: providing supplementary feed in the dry season only

questions.explanation: Rotational grazing divides pasture into paddocks, rotating animals through them systematically to allow each paddock to regrow before being grazed again, preventing overgrazing and maintaining pasture productivity.

questions.question: Overgrazing leads to all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: reduced pasture productivity

questions.explanation: Overgrazing removes excessive vegetation, exposing soil to erosion, depleting pasture plants, compacting soil, reducing water infiltration, and eventually causing desertification. It does not improve soil fertility.

questions.question: Early blight of tomato is caused by the fungus:

options.B: Alternaria solani

questions.explanation: Early blight of tomato is caused by Alternaria solani, producing characteristic target-like (concentric ring) brown spots on lower leaves, causing defoliation and yield loss.

questions.question: Black pod disease of cocoa is caused by the pathogen:

options.B: Phytophthora megakarya

questions.explanation: Black pod disease (caused by Phytophthora species, mainly P. megakarya in West Africa) is the most devastating cocoa disease, causing pods to turn black and rot, causing 30-90% yield losses.

questions.question: Damping off in seedlings is caused by:

options.B: soil-borne fungi (Pythium, Rhizoctonia, Fusarium) that rot the stem at soil level

questions.explanation: Damping off is a fungal nursery disease (caused by Pythium, Rhizoctonia, Fusarium) where seedling stems rot at soil level, causing collapse. It is favored by wet, poorly drained soils.

questions.question: Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs) in agriculture are crops that have been:

options.B: developed by inserting specific genes from one organism into another to give desired traits

questions.explanation: GMOs are created using recombinant DNA technology to insert specific genes conferring desired traits (pest resistance, drought tolerance, herbicide resistance, improved nutrition) into crop plants.

questions.question: Tissue culture in plant propagation is used to:

options.B: rapidly multiply disease-free planting material from small amounts of plant tissue in sterile conditions

questions.explanation: Plant tissue culture (micropropagation) rapidly produces thousands of disease-free, genetically uniform plantlets from small pieces of tissue in sterile laboratory conditions — used for banana, oil palm, cassava.

questions.question: Agricultural mechanization in Nigeria is limited mainly by:

options.B: high cost of machinery, small farm sizes, and lack of skilled operators and spare parts

questions.explanation: Agricultural mechanization in Nigeria is constrained by: high cost of tractors and equipment, small fragmented farm holdings, poor maintenance culture, lack of skilled operators, and inadequate fuel supply.

questions.question: Apiculture is the practice of:

options.B: snail farming
options.C: beekeeping for honey, beeswax, and pollination services

questions.explanation: Apiculture (beekeeping) involves the management of bee colonies to produce honey, beeswax, propolis, and royal jelly, and to provide pollination services for crops.

questions.question: Snail farming (heliculture) in Nigeria is becoming popular because:

options.B: snails are a cheap source of protein, easy to raise, and require minimal inputs
options.D: snails are a major export commodity

questions.explanation: Heliculture is gaining popularity as snails (Archachatina marginata, Achatina achatina) are a cheap protein source, easy to raise in small spaces, require minimal capital, and have a ready market.

questions.question: Rabbits are excellent for small-scale farming because:

options.A: they are ruminants with specialized digestion
options.B: they reproduce rapidly, require small space, and provide lean protein-rich meat
options.C: they are nocturnal and require no management
options.D: they produce large quantities of milk

questions.explanation: Rabbits are ideal for small-scale farming: they reproduce rapidly (litter of 5-12 kits every 30-35 days), require minimal space and feed, produce high-quality lean meat, and have low startup costs.

questions.question: The Land Use Act of 1978 in Nigeria vested ownership of all land in Nigeria in:

options.B: the State governments, which hold land in trust for the people

questions.explanation: The Land Use Act of 1978 vested all land in Nigeria in State Governors, who hold it in trust. Individuals and organizations can only obtain rights of occupancy (Certificates of Occupancy — C of O) from the state.

questions.question: Land fragmentation (small, scattered farm plots) in Nigeria is a problem because it:

options.B: makes it difficult to use machinery, reduces efficiency, and discourages investment

questions.explanation: Land fragmentation creates small, scattered plots that prevent mechanization, waste time in moving between plots, reduce economies of scale, and discourage farm investment and improvement.

questions.question: Fermentation is used in food processing to:

options.B: convert sugars to alcohol and organic acids by microorganisms, improving flavor and preservation

questions.explanation: Fermentation uses microorganisms (bacteria, yeasts, moulds) to transform food biochemically, improving flavor, texture, nutritional value, and shelf life. Used for ogi, ugba, dawadawa, ogi, and palm wine in Nigeria.

questions.question: Sun drying of fish and agricultural produce is a simple preservation method that works by:

options.B: removing moisture to inhibit microbial growth and enzymatic activity

questions.explanation: Sun drying removes moisture from food (reduces water activity), creating conditions unfavourable for bacterial and fungal growth, thereby extending shelf life. It is widely used for fish, meat, vegetables, and grains in Nigeria.

questions.question: Agroforestry is a land-use system that:

options.A: grows trees in place of crops
options.B: deliberately combines trees or shrubs with crops and/or livestock on the same land

questions.explanation: Agroforestry intentionally integrates trees with crops and/or livestock to provide multiple benefits: shade, windbreaks, nitrogen fixation, timber, fruits, fodder, and improved soil fertility.

questions.question: Seed viability refers to:

options.A: the weight of seeds per kilogram
options.B: the ability of a seed to germinate and produce a normal seedling

questions.explanation: Seed viability is the capacity of a seed to germinate and produce a normal healthy seedling under favorable conditions. Viability decreases with age, improper storage, and pest or disease damage.

questions.question: Seed dormancy refers to a state where seeds:

options.B: fail to germinate even under favorable conditions due to internal physiological factors
options.D: need to be soaked in water before planting

questions.explanation: Seed dormancy is a condition where viable seeds remain dormant and fail to germinate even when environmental conditions (water, temperature, oxygen) are favorable. It prevents premature germination.

questions.question: In southern Nigeria, there are typically:

options.B: two planting seasons (early and late rains)

questions.explanation: Southern Nigeria has two distinct rainfall peaks, allowing two cropping seasons: the early (first) rains season (March-July) and the late (second) rains season (September-November).

questions.question: In northern Nigeria, crop production is mainly limited to:

options.B: one rainy season (May-September) with possible dry-season irrigation farming (fadama)

questions.explanation: Northern Nigeria has one rainy season (May-September) allowing one main cropping season. In the dry season, fadama farming (irrigated cultivation along floodplains and water sources) is practiced.

questions.question: Vegetative propagation is preferred over seed propagation for some crops because it:

options.B: produces plants identical to the parent (clones), maintains variety characteristics, and gives earlier bearing

questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation (cuttings, grafting, budding, suckers) produces genetically identical plants (clones) that maintain the parent's desired traits and often bear fruit earlier than seed-grown plants.

questions.question: Grafting in fruit tree propagation involves:

options.B: joining a scion (desired variety) to a rootstock (disease-resistant root system) to combine their best traits
options.C: laying branches on the soil to root

questions.explanation: Grafting joins a scion (shoot from desired variety for fruit quality) onto a rootstock (different plant with desirable root traits like vigor, disease resistance). Used for citrus, mango, and cocoa.

questions.question: Budding is a form of grafting that uses:

options.A: an entire shoot (scion) joined to a rootstock
options.B: a single bud from the desired variety inserted under the bark of the rootstock

questions.explanation: Budding (T-budding, chip budding) inserts a single bud from the desired variety under the bark of the rootstock. It is simpler and uses less scion material than full grafting. Used for roses, citrus, and rubber.

questions.question: Compost is produced by:

options.B: decomposing organic materials (crop residues, animal manure, kitchen waste) through microbial action

questions.explanation: Compost is made by piling organic materials (plant residues, manure, food waste) and allowing them to decompose through microbial activity, producing a humus-rich organic fertilizer.

questions.question: For effective composting, the compost heap should contain:

options.B: a mixture of carbon-rich (brown) and nitrogen-rich (green) materials, moisture, and air

questions.explanation: Effective composting requires a balance of carbon-rich (dry leaves, straw) and nitrogen-rich (fresh plant material, animal manure) materials, adequate moisture (like a wrung-out sponge), and oxygen for aerobic decomposition.

questions.question: Sugar cane (Saccharum officinarum) is propagated by:

options.B: stem cuttings (setts) containing nodal buds

questions.explanation: Sugarcane is propagated vegetatively using stem cuttings (setts) — pieces of the mature stalk with 2-3 buds (nodes) that sprout to form new plants.

questions.question: Pasteurization of milk involves:

options.B: heating milk to 72°C for 15 seconds (HTST) to kill pathogenic bacteria while retaining nutritional quality

questions.explanation: Pasteurization kills pathogenic bacteria (TB, Brucella, Salmonella) in milk by heating to 72°C for 15 seconds (HTST) or 63°C for 30 minutes (LTLT), extending shelf life and improving safety.

questions.question: Biological nitrogen fixation in legume crops is carried out by the bacteria:

options.B: Rhizobium

questions.explanation: Rhizobium bacteria form symbiotic root nodules in legumes, fixing atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia (NH₃) that the plant can use. In return, the plant provides carbohydrates to the bacteria.

questions.question: Earthworms are beneficial to soil and agriculture because they:

options.B: improve soil aeration, drainage, and fertility by burrowing and breaking down organic matter

questions.explanation: Earthworms improve soil structure by creating channels (improving aeration and drainage), mixing soil layers, breaking down organic matter into humus, and producing nutrient-rich castings.

questions.question: Micronutrients (trace elements) are nutrients required by plants in:

options.B: very small amounts but are essential for normal plant growth

questions.explanation: Micronutrients (Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl, Ni) are required in tiny amounts (parts per million) but are essential for specific enzyme functions, chlorophyll synthesis, and other metabolic processes.

questions.question: Zinc deficiency in maize causes a symptom known as:

options.B: White bud (bleaching of young leaves) and stunting

questions.explanation: Zinc deficiency in maize causes 'white bud' — young leaves emerge pale white/yellow with striped pattern, shortened internodes, and stunted growth. Zinc is critical for auxin synthesis and enzyme activation.

questions.question: Horses in Nigeria are used mainly for:

options.B: transportation, ceremonial purposes, and polo sport, mainly in northern Nigeria

questions.explanation: Horses in Nigeria are used for transportation (riding), ceremonial durbar festivals, polo sport, and traditional ceremonies mainly in the northern states. They are not major livestock for meat or dairy.

questions.question: Vernalization in plants refers to the process where:

options.A: plants are treated with pesticides to prevent disease
options.B: exposure to low temperatures promotes flowering in certain plant species
options.C: plants are acclimated to high temperatures
options.D: nutrients are absorbed more efficiently in cold weather

questions.explanation: Vernalization is the promotion of flowering by prolonged cold temperature exposure. Some temperate cereals (wheat) and biennial plants require cold treatment to switch from vegetative to reproductive growth.

questions.question: The practice of pricking out in the nursery involves:

options.A: using pins to mark seedling positions
options.B: transferring seedlings from their initial germination tray to a more spacious container

questions.explanation: Pricking out is the transfer of young seedlings from the germination tray/flat to a larger container or nursery bed to give them more space to grow before final transplanting to the field.

questions.question: Biogas production from animal waste (biodigester) is beneficial because it:

options.B: produces renewable energy (methane) and organic slurry for use as fertilizer
options.C: eliminates the need for crop rotation

questions.explanation: Biodigesters convert animal manure and organic waste into biogas (methane for cooking/lighting) and bioslurry (nutrient-rich organic fertilizer), providing clean energy and improving farm waste management.

questions.question: The optimum planting depth for maize seeds is approximately:

options.B: 3-5 cm

questions.explanation: Maize seeds should be planted at 3-5 cm depth — shallow enough for the seedling to emerge easily but deep enough to access moisture and be anchored in the soil.

questions.question: Topping of tobacco plants is done to:

options.B: remove the terminal bud/flowers to redirect energy to leaf development and improve leaf quality
options.C: mark the tallest plants for seed saving
options.D: allow the plant to flower and set seed

questions.explanation: Topping in tobacco removes the terminal flower bud to prevent seed formation, redirecting the plant's energy to the leaves, increasing their size, thickness, and nicotine content.

questions.question: The main purpose of providing housing for farm animals is to:

options.A: keep animals away from human habitation only
options.B: protect animals from extreme weather, predators, and disease, while facilitating management
options.C: reduce the amount of feed needed

questions.explanation: Animal housing protects livestock from rain, sun, cold, and predators; facilitates feeding, watering, treatment, and record-keeping; and prevents disease transmission by controlling the environment.

questions.question: A deep litter system for poultry uses bedding material (wood shavings, rice husks) that is:

options.B: kept in the house throughout the production cycle, removed only at cleanout

questions.explanation: In the deep litter system, bedding (8-10 cm of wood shavings, rice husks, or straw) accumulates throughout the production cycle. Managed properly (kept dry, turned regularly), it generates heat and controls pathogens.

questions.question: Crop yield is typically expressed as:

options.B: weight of produce per unit area (kg/ha or tonnes/ha)
options.D: total weight of all farms in a country

questions.explanation: Crop yield is the amount of produce (grain, tubers, fruits) harvested per unit area of land, typically expressed as kilograms per hectare (kg/ha) or tonnes per hectare (t/ha).

questions.question: Rubber (Hevea brasiliensis) is cultivated in Nigeria mainly in:

options.B: Edo, Delta, Cross River, and Ondo states in the southern forest zone

questions.explanation: Rubber cultivation in Nigeria is concentrated in the rainforest belt, particularly in Edo, Delta, Cross River, and Ondo states, where the humid tropical climate suits Hevea brasiliensis.

questions.question: Latex (raw rubber) is collected from the rubber tree by the process of:

options.B: tapping — making incisions in the bark to collect the latex sap

questions.explanation: Rubber tapping involves making a diagonal incision in the tree's bark down to the cambium. Latex (milky sap) flows out and is collected in cups. Tapping is done in the cool early morning.

questions.question: Strip cropping as a soil conservation technique involves:

options.A: growing one crop across the entire field
options.B: alternating strips of different crops or grass across a slope to slow erosion
options.C: removing all vegetation from the field

questions.explanation: Strip cropping alternates strips of erosion-susceptible crops (maize, cotton) with erosion-resistant strips (grass, legumes) across slopes, reducing runoff velocity and soil loss.

questions.question: Cover cropping involves growing:

options.B: ground-covering plants to protect the soil from erosion, improve fertility, and suppress weeds

questions.explanation: Cover crops (cowpea, mucuna, sweet potato) are grown to protect bare soil from erosion, add organic matter, fix nitrogen (legumes), suppress weeds, and improve soil structure.

questions.question: Windbreaks (shelterbelts) are planted in farms to:

options.B: reduce wind speed, protect crops from wind damage, and prevent wind erosion

questions.explanation: Windbreaks are rows of trees or shrubs planted perpendicular to prevailing winds to reduce wind speed, protect crops from wind damage and desiccation, prevent wind erosion, and provide habitat for wildlife.

questions.question: The Holstein-Friesian cattle breed is known for:

options.B: the highest milk production among all cattle breeds

questions.explanation: Holstein-Friesian (black and white cows from Holland) are the world's highest milk-producing dairy breed, producing 8,000-12,000 litres per lactation under ideal conditions.

questions.question: The N'Dama cattle breed is important in West Africa because of its:

options.B: trypanotolerance — natural resistance to sleeping sickness (trypanosomiasis)

questions.explanation: The N'Dama is a West African Bos taurus breed renowned for its trypanotolerance — genetic resistance to trypanosomiasis — making it valuable in tsetse-infested humid zones where exotic breeds cannot survive.

questions.question: The Rhode Island Red is a dual-purpose poultry breed that is:

options.B: used only for egg production
options.C: used for both egg and meat production and is adaptable to local conditions

questions.explanation: Rhode Island Red is an American dual-purpose breed that produces reasonable quantities of both eggs and meat. It is hardy, adaptable to village conditions, and popular in small-scale farming.

questions.question: Vaccination of farm animals is done to:

options.A: treat animals already infected with diseases
options.B: stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies and prevent specific diseases
options.D: improve the growth rate of animals

questions.explanation: Vaccination stimulates the animal's immune system to produce antibodies against specific pathogens, providing protection (immunity) against that disease without causing the illness.

questions.question: Drenching of livestock involves:

options.B: administering liquid medicine orally using a drench gun or bottle

questions.explanation: Drenching is the oral administration of liquid medicines (anthelmintics for deworming, mineral supplements, vitamins) to livestock using a drenching gun or improvised bottle.

questions.question: Deworming of livestock is done to control:

options.B: internal parasites (roundworms, tapeworms, flukes) in the gastrointestinal tract

questions.explanation: Deworming (using anthelmintics like albendazole, ivermectin) controls gastrointestinal and other internal parasites (roundworms, tapeworms, flukes, lungworms) that reduce animal productivity and health.

questions.question: Horticulture is the branch of agriculture that deals with:

options.B: cultivation of vegetables, fruits, flowers, and ornamental plants

questions.explanation: Horticulture covers the cultivation of fruit crops (pomology), vegetables (olericulture), flowers and ornamentals (floriculture), and landscape plants. It involves intensive, high-value crop production.

questions.question: Pruning of fruit trees is done to:

options.B: remove dead, diseased, or overcrowded branches to improve light penetration, air circulation, and fruit production
options.C: prevent the tree from growing too tall only

questions.explanation: Pruning removes dead, diseased, crossing, or surplus branches to: improve light distribution and air circulation within the canopy, reduce disease incidence, encourage new growth, and improve fruit size and quality.

questions.question: Tomato production in Nigeria is limited mainly by:

options.B: high rainfall destroying crops
options.C: pests and diseases, post-harvest losses, and seasonal price fluctuations

questions.explanation: Tomato production in Nigeria faces challenges including: pests (tomato worm, thrips), diseases (early and late blight, bacterial wilt), massive post-harvest losses (up to 45%), and infrastructure/storage inadequacy.

questions.question: Sorghum (Sorghum bicolor) is important in northern Nigeria because:

options.B: it is drought-tolerant and heat-resistant, suitable for semi-arid conditions
options.D: it is only used for animal feed

questions.explanation: Sorghum is well adapted to the dry northern conditions — it is drought-tolerant, heat-resistant, and can produce grain even with limited rainfall, making it a staple in Nigeria's semi-arid north.

questions.question: Cowpea (Vigna unguiculata) is an important food crop in Nigeria because it:

options.B: is a high-protein legume that fixes nitrogen, grows in poor soils, and is drought-tolerant

questions.explanation: Cowpea (black-eyed pea) is Nigeria's most important grain legume — it provides high-quality protein (23-25%), fixes atmospheric nitrogen improving soil fertility, grows in poor sandy soils, and tolerates drought.

questions.question: Fadama farming in northern Nigeria refers to:

options.A: dry season farming in the Sahara
options.B: irrigated cultivation on seasonally flooded low-lying lands along river valleys

questions.explanation: Fadama refers to low-lying, seasonally flooded plains along river valleys in northern Nigeria. Fadama farming uses shallow groundwater or surface water for dry-season vegetable, rice, and crop production.

questions.question: Rice-fish farming (integrated rice-fish culture) is practiced because:

options.A: fish destroy rice weeds and pests while rice provides cover and food for fish
options.B: fish dry out the rice paddies
options.C: rice improves fish survival in dry conditions

questions.explanation: In rice-fish integration, fish (tilapia, carp) eat weeds, insects, and pests in the paddy, reducing weed/pest control costs; their excrement fertilizes rice; rice provides shelter and food for fish — a mutually beneficial system.

questions.question: Turkeys are reared primarily for:

options.B: meat production — they are one of the largest domesticated poultry species

questions.explanation: Turkeys are reared primarily for meat. They are the largest domesticated poultry birds, with commercial breeds reaching 15-25 kg at market weight.

questions.question: An Agricultural Development Programme (ADP) in Nigeria is a government initiative to:

options.B: provide extension services, inputs, and infrastructure to small-scale farmers to improve productivity

questions.explanation: Agricultural Development Programmes (ADPs) established in each state in the 1970s-80s aimed to reach small-scale farmers with extension services, improved seeds, fertilizers, credit, and rural infrastructure.

questions.question: Food security exists when:

options.A: a country exports all its food
options.B: all people at all times have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food
options.C: a country has emergency food reserves only
options.D: only wealthy citizens can afford food

questions.explanation: The FAO defines food security as existing when all people, at all times, have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food for an active and healthy life.

questions.question: A seedbed is:

options.A: a bed of manure for composting
options.B: prepared soil made fine and firm, ready for seed germination and early seedling growth
options.C: the area where seeds are stored

questions.explanation: A seedbed is soil that has been carefully prepared (ploughed, harrowed, levelled) to create a fine, firm, moist, and well-aerated surface suitable for sowing seeds and early plant establishment.

questions.question: Hardening off of seedlings refers to:

options.A: applying chemical growth regulators to make stems tough
options.B: gradually acclimatizing seedlings grown in a nursery to outdoor conditions before transplanting
options.C: allowing seedlings to dry slightly before watering

questions.explanation: Hardening off gradually acclimatizes tender nursery-raised seedlings to outdoor conditions (reduced watering, increased sun exposure, wind) over 1-2 weeks, reducing transplanting shock.

questions.question: When using chemical pesticides on the farm, it is essential to:

options.A: mix pesticides with bare hands for better distribution
options.B: wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) including gloves, mask, and goggles
options.C: apply pesticides on windy days for wider coverage
options.D: spray near water sources for convenience

questions.explanation: Pesticide use requires: full PPE (gloves, mask, goggles, overalls, boots), reading the label, correct mixing, avoiding application on windy days, and keeping pesticides away from water sources and food.

questions.question: The recommended spacing for maize planting in Nigeria (75cm × 25cm with 2 plants per stand) gives a plant population of approximately:

options.B: 53,000 plants/ha

questions.explanation: Plant population = 10,000 m² / (0.75m × 0.25m) × 2 plants = 10,000/0.1875 × 2 = approximately 53,333 plants/ha. This is the standard recommended maize density in Nigeria.

questions.question: The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) is an agency of the United Nations that works to:

options.B: achieve food security for all and ensure regular access to high-quality food globally

questions.explanation: FAO leads international efforts to defeat hunger, improve nutrition, and ensure food security. It provides data, technical assistance, and policy guidance to member countries.

questions.question: Ad libitum feeding in animal production means:

options.A: feeding animals according to a strict schedule
options.B: allowing animals free access to feed at all times
options.C: restricted feeding to control body weight

questions.explanation: Ad libitum (ad lib) feeding allows animals continuous free access to feed. It maximizes growth rate but requires monitoring to prevent waste and obesity in some species.

questions.question: A nursery in crop production is a place where:

options.B: young seedlings are raised under controlled conditions before transplanting to the field
options.C: farm animals are born and reared

questions.explanation: A plant nursery is a facility where seeds are germinated and seedlings raised under controlled conditions (shade, irrigation, fertilization) until they are strong enough to be transplanted to the field.

questions.question: Castration of male farm animals is practiced to:

options.B: reduce aggressive behavior, improve meat quality, and facilitate management

questions.explanation: Castration (removal or destruction of testes) of male animals reduces aggression and fighting, improves feed conversion and meat quality (less tough, better marbling), and facilitates mixed-sex group management.

questions.question: Dehorning of cattle is practiced to:

options.B: prevent cattle from injuring each other and handlers, and reduce carcass bruising
options.D: facilitate horn growth in improved breeds

questions.explanation: Dehorning prevents cattle from injuring other animals (causing wounds, abscesses, even death) and handlers, reduces feed trough space needed, and reduces carcass bruising during transport.

questions.question: The main advantage of using certified seeds over farm-saved seeds is that certified seeds:

options.B: are tested for purity, viability, disease freedom, and have known, improved performance

questions.explanation: Certified seeds have been tested and approved for genetic purity, physical purity, germination rate, and freedom from seed-borne diseases. They give predictable, improved performance compared to farm-saved seeds.

questions.question: The economic threshold in pest management is defined as:

options.A: the maximum number of pests that can be found on any crop
options.B: the pest population level at which the cost of control equals the economic damage caused by the pest
options.C: the total cost of pesticide application
options.D: the number of beneficial insects in the field

questions.explanation: The Economic Threshold (ET) is the pest density at which control measures are economically justified — when the cost of the pest damage exceeds the cost of control. This guides rational pesticide use.

questions.question: A hectare (ha) is a unit of area equal to:

options.B: 10,000 square metres (100m × 100m)

questions.explanation: 1 hectare = 10,000 m² (a square 100m × 100m). 1 ha ≈ 2.47 acres. It is the standard unit of land area in agriculture. 1 km² = 100 hectares.

questions.question: The cation exchange capacity (CEC) of a soil refers to:

options.A: the soil's ability to drain water
options.B: the soil's capacity to hold and exchange positively charged nutrient ions (cations)
options.C: the rate at which soils weather

questions.explanation: CEC is the total capacity of soil to hold exchangeable cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺, NH₄⁺, etc.). Soils with high CEC (clays, organic matter) hold more nutrients and are potentially more fertile.

questions.question: The C:N ratio (carbon to nitrogen ratio) of organic matter affects:

options.B: the rate of decomposition and nutrient availability in the soil

questions.explanation: C:N ratio determines decomposition rate. Low C:N (<20:1, like legume residues) decomposes quickly, releasing N; high C:N (>30:1, like straw, wood) decomposes slowly and may cause N immobilization.

questions.question: Zero tillage (no-till) farming is practiced to:

options.B: conserve soil moisture, reduce erosion, and save fuel and labor by not ploughing

questions.explanation: Zero/no-till farming avoids soil disturbance, conserving soil moisture, reducing erosion, maintaining soil organic matter and structure, reducing fuel costs, and preserving beneficial soil organisms.

questions.question: The primary function of the cotyledon(s) in a seed is to:

options.A: protect the embryo from physical damage
options.B: provide stored food (starch, protein, fat) for the germinating seedling until it can photosynthesize

questions.explanation: Cotyledons (seed leaves) are the food storage organs of seeds. They supply the germinating seedling with energy and nutrients until it develops true leaves and can photosynthesize independently.

questions.question: A monocotyledonous plant has seeds with:

options.B: one seed leaf

questions.explanation: Monocots (e.g., maize, sorghum, rice, wheat, onion, grass) have one cotyledon (seed leaf). Dicots (e.g., bean, tomato, groundnut, cowpea) have two cotyledons.

questions.question: Sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) is propagated by:

options.B: vine cuttings (slips from the storage roots) or stem cuttings

questions.explanation: Sweet potato is propagated vegetatively using vine cuttings (slips sprouted from the storage root) or stem/vine cuttings. It is rarely grown from true seed except in breeding programs.

questions.question: Soybean (Glycine max) is an important crop in Nigeria because:

options.A: it is a major starchy food crop
options.B: it is a high-protein (40%), high-oil (20%) legume used for food, feed, and industrial products
options.C: it is the main cocoa substitute

questions.explanation: Soybean is Nigeria's most important oilseed crop, rich in protein (40%) and oil (20%). It is used for human food (soy milk, tofu), animal feed, vegetable oil, and as raw material for various industries.

questions.question: Hide and skin from cattle and goats are used primarily for:

options.B: leather production for shoes, bags, and other leather goods

questions.explanation: Hide (from cattle) and skin (from goats and sheep) are processed into leather used for manufacturing shoes, belts, handbags, furniture, and other leather products. Nigeria is a major exporter of hides and skins.

questions.question: Nematodes (roundworms) as plant pests cause damage to crops by:

options.B: parasitizing roots — causing root knots, galls, and disrupting water and nutrient uptake

questions.explanation: Plant-parasitic nematodes (Meloidogyne root-knot nematodes) penetrate roots, causing galls (swellings) that disrupt water/nutrient uptake, leading to stunting, yellowing, and yield losses.

questions.question: The major benefit of practicing conservation agriculture is:

options.B: sustainable long-term productivity by maintaining soil health, reducing erosion and input costs
options.C: higher short-term yields at the expense of soil health

questions.explanation: Conservation agriculture (minimum tillage, permanent soil cover, crop rotation) improves long-term soil health, reduces erosion and degradation, conserves water and organic matter, and lowers production costs.
